content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
April 26,
1994
Richard K. Denhalter
City Attorney
City of San Juan Capistrano
32400 Paseo Adelanto
San Juan Capistrano,
CA
92675
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—94—139
Dear Mr.
Denhalter:
This is in response to your letter requesting clarification
with respect to the Nash Advice Letter,
No.
A-93-481
(issued on
January 11,
1994)
regarding Councilmember Carolyn Nash and her
responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the “Act”) •1
The facts in the Nash Advice Letter were as follows:
Councilmember Nash’s spouse is the director of research for a
nonprofit entity, the San Juan Capistrano Research Institute
(the
“institute”).
She inquired as to whether her interest in the
institute would create a conflict of interest.
We advised that
she would have a conflict of interest in decisions that will have
a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the
institute.
You have asked specifically how materiality would be
determined with respect to the institute.
As we stated on page
6
of the Nash Advice Letter,
Regulation 18702.5 should be applied to
determine materiality.
On that same page,
we provided an example
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless othefwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-94-139
Page
2
of how the regulation would be applied “if the institute’s gross
annual receipts are $100,000 or less.. .“
You stated in your letter of April 18,
1994, that
Councilmember Nash has indicated to you that the gross annual
receipts of the institute was actually $479,000 in 1993.
You have
asked us to confirm that, under those facts, Regulation 18702.5(e)
is the appropriate materiality standard.
Regulation 18702.5(e)
provides that an effect is considered material under the following
circumstances:
For an entity whose gross annual receipts are
more than $100,000 but less than or equal to
$1,000,000 the effect of the decision will be any
of the following:
(1)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease of the entity’s gross annual receipts
for a fiscal year in the amount of $50,000 or more.
(2)
The decision will cause the entity to
incur or avoid additional expenses or to reduce or
eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year in
the amount of $12,500 or more.
(3)
The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of the entity’s assets or
liabilities in the amount of $50,000 or more.
According to your facts, this provision would apply to the
institute.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322_5901.2
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
.1 Counsel
By:\ John W. Wallace
Counsel,
Legal Division
2
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10 per page.

sections.question: How would materiality be determined with respect to the San Juan Capistrano Research Institute under Regulation 18702.5, given that the institute's gross annual receipts were $479,000 in 1993?
sections.conclusion: Regulation 18702.5(e) is the appropriate materiality standard. For an entity with gross annual receipts more than $100,000 but less than or equal to $1,000,000, a decision has a material financial effect if it: (1) results in an increase or decrease of gross annual receipts of $50,000 or more; (2) causes the entity to incur or avoid additional expenses or reduce existing expenses of $12,500 or more; or (3) results in an increase or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of $50,000 or more.
sections.question_synthetic: The City Attorney asks for clarification on how to apply the materiality standard under Regulation 18702.5 to determine if Councilmember Nash has a conflict of interest regarding a nonprofit institute where her spouse works, given the institute's actual annual receipts of $479,000.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirms that Regulation 18702.5(e) applies to the institute based on its $479,000 gross annual receipts, establishing specific dollar thresholds for determining whether decisions would have a material financial effect on the institute.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a follow-up advice letter clarifying a prior advice letter (A-93-481). The document clearly presents the question and conclusion regarding materiality determination. No explicit 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers, but content is well-structured and unambiguous.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How would materiality be determined with respect to the San Juan Capistrano Research Institute under Regulation 18702.5, given that the institute's gross annual receipts were $479,000 in 1993?

CONCLUSION: Regulation 18702.5(e) is the appropriate materiality standard. For an entity with gross annual receipts more than $100,000 but less than or equal to $1,000,000, a decision has a material financial effect if it: (1) results in an increase or decrease of gross annual receipts of $50,000 or more; (2) causes the entity to incur or avoid additional expenses or reduce existing expenses of $12,500 or more; or (3) results in an increase or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of $50,000 or more.

embedding.summary: The FPPC clarifies the materiality standard applicable to Councilmember Nash's conflict-of-interest determination regarding the San Juan Capistrano Research Institute, confirming that Regulation 18702.5(e) applies given the institute's $479,000 in annual gross receipts.
